## 0. The reference implementations (ranked)

| Repo | Lang | Last push | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **evanpurkhiser/things3-cloud** | Rust | **2026-07-15** | Best modern reference. Full read+write CLI: tasks, projects, headings, checklists, tags, areas, recurrence parsing, notes (plain + structured), reorder, trash. Cleanest wire-format model (`src/wire/*`). |
| **arthursoares/things-cloud-sdk** | Go | **2026-07-07** | Maintained continuation of nicolai86's SDK. Full read+write incl. recurring tasks, notes delta patches, device/APNS registration, SQLite sync engine. Its `docs/client-side-bugs.md` is the single most valuable protocol document — HAR-verified against real Things 3.15 traffic, documents crash-inducing pitfalls. |
| nicolai86/things-cloud-sdk | Go | 2022 | The original reverse-engineering (110 stars). Read+write, repeat rules, memory state. Superseded by arthursoares fork. |
| disrupted/things-cloud-api | Python | 2025-03 | WIP, Task6-only (no checklist write, no areas/tags write). Useful as Python/pydantic field mapping and for the alternate `getT3SharedSession` auth endpoint. On PyPI as `things-cloud`. |
| mattydsmith / wbopan things-cloud-mcp | Go | 2026-03 | MCP server built on the Go SDK; nothing protocol-new. |
| m1guelpf/things-cloud-api | Rust | 2024 | Minor. |

Raw files worth fetching yourself:
- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/evanpurkhiser/things3-cloud/main/src/client.rs (endpoints, headers, pagination, commit)
- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/evanpurkhiser/things3-cloud/main/src/wire/task.rs (complete Task6 field map)
- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/arthursoares/things-cloud-sdk/main/docs/client-side-bugs.md (HAR-verified semantics, `st` matrix, Base58 UUID trap, pagination trap)
- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/arthursoares/things-cloud-sdk/main/histories.go (history CRUD + commit headers)

## 1. Authentication

Base URL: `https://cloud.culturedcode.com/version/1` (the old `thingscloud.appspot.com` GAE domain only survives in ancient SLA URLs; everything is on cloud.culturedcode.com now).

**Login = GET account, password in header** (no session/token):

```
GET /version/1/account/{urlencoded-email}
Authorization: Password {urlencoded-password}
```

200 response (disrupted `api/account.py`, nicolai86 `verify.go`):

```json
{
  "status": "SYAccountStatusActive",
  "SLA-version-accepted": "https://cloud.culturedcode.com/sla/...",
  "email": "you@example.com",
  "maildrop-email": "add-to-things-xxxx@things.email",
  "issues": [],
  "history-key": "<uuid-v4>"
}
```

`history-key` is the only credential-derived value you need — it goes into all subsequent URLs, which are otherwise **unauthenticated** (arthursoares Bug 7 notes `/items` can be curl'd with no auth; the history key is the secret).

Headers sent by current clients (things3-cloud `src/client.rs`, mimicking Things Mac 3.22):

```
User-Agent: ThingsMac/32209501
App-Id: com.culturedcode.ThingsMac
App-Instance-Id: <arbitrary id; real app uses {64-hex}-com.culturedcode.ThingsMac-{64-hex}; "-com.culturedcode.ThingsMac" also works>
Schema: 301
Accept: application/json
Accept-Charset: UTF-8
Content-Type: application/json; charset=UTF-8   (on POST)
Content-Encoding: UTF-8                          (on POST; yes, literally)
things-client-info: <base64 JSON>               (optional; decodes to
  {"dm":"Mac14,2","lr":"US","nf":true,"nk":true,"nn":"ThingsMac","nv":"32209501",
   "on":"macOS","ov":"26.3.0","pl":"en-US","ul":"en-Latn-US"})
```

Other account endpoints (nicolai86/arthursoares `account.go`, `histories.go`): `PUT /account/{email}` with `{"password": ...}` = signup (201), with `{"confirmation-code": ...}` = confirm, with `{"SLA-version-accepted": ...}` = accept SLA, `DELETE /account/{email}` = delete (202); `GET|POST|DELETE /account/{email}/own-history-keys[/{key}]` = list/create/delete histories (create returns `{"new-history-key": ...}`).

Alternate session endpoint (disrupted `api/account.py`): `POST https://cloud.culturedcode.com/api/account/login/getT3SharedSession` with `Authorization: B64SON <base64 of {"ep":{"e":email,"p":password}}>` → `{"headIndex": N, "historyKeySessionSecret": "..."}`. Optional — the Rust/Go clients don't need it.

## 2. History / sync model

The history is an **append-only event journal**; each committed item has a monotonic server index. Client state = fold of all items.

```
GET /version/1/history/{history-key}            → {"latest-server-index": N, "latest-schema-version": 301, "is-empty": bool, "latest-total-content-size": N}
GET /version/1/history/{history-key}/items?start-index={i}
```

Items response:

```json
{
  "items": [ { "<uuid>": {"t":0, "e":"Task6", "p":{...}}, "<uuid2>": {...} }, ... ],
  "current-item-index": 201,
  "schema": 301,
  "start-total-content-size": 0,
  "end-total-content-size": 12345,
  "latest-total-content-size": 99999
}
```

- `items` is an array of maps; **one array element = one server commit** and may contain multiple UUIDs.
- Pagination, two working strategies: (a) things3-cloud: loop `start_index += len(items array)` until `end-total-content-size >= latest-total-content-size`; (b) arthursoares (post-Bug-7 fix): set next `start-index` to the response's `current-item-index`. **Do not** advance by the count of expanded per-UUID entities — `start-index > current-item-index` makes the server return **500**.
- Current schema version: **301** (send as `Schema: 301` header; server echoes as `"schema"` in the items response). Unchanged since ~2017 across all repos.
- Applying: fold in order; `t:0` replaces object state for UUID, `t:1` shallow-merges `p` into it, and JSON `null` in a patch **clears** the field (things3-cloud models this with double-Option — you must distinguish "absent" from "null").

## 3. Item / delta wire format

Every entry: `{"<base58-uuid>": {"t": <op>, "e": "<EntityType>", "p": {…fields…}}}`.

**Ops (`t`)**: `0` = create (full snapshot), `1` = update (sparse patch), `2` = delete.

**Entity types (`e`)**, current versions in bold (things3-cloud `wire_object.rs`): **`Task6`** (also legacy Task3/Task4) — covers todos, projects, AND headings, discriminated by `p.tp`; **`ChecklistItem3`** (legacy ChecklistItem, ChecklistItem2); **`Tag4`** (legacy Tag3); **`Area3`** (legacy Area2); **`Tombstone2`** (legacy Tombstone); `Settings3/4/5` (ignore); `Command`.

**Task6 fields** (complete map, from `wire/task.rs` + disrupted `models/todo.py`):

| Key | Meaning | Type |
|---|---|---|
| `tt` | title | string |
| `nt` | notes — legacy plain string/XML, or structured `{"_t":"tx","t":1,"ch":0,"v":"full text"}` (t=2 = paragraph list `ps:[{"r":...}]`; delta note patches also exist, see arthursoares `notes.go`) |
| `tp` | type: 0=Todo, 1=Project, 2=Heading | int |
| `ss` | status: 0=Incomplete, 2=Canceled, 3=Completed | int |
| `sp` | stop (completion/cancel) timestamp | float\|null |
| `st` | start state: 0=Inbox/"not started", 1=started (Anytime; **Today when sr/tir = today**), 2=deferred (Someday; Upcoming when sr future) | int |
| `sr` | scheduled/start day | int day-timestamp\|null |
| `tir` | "today index reference" day — set equal to `sr` for Today items | int\|null |
| `dd` | deadline day | int\|null |
| `dds` | deadline-suppression day | usually null |
| `pr` | parent project UUIDs (0–1) | array |
| `ar` | area UUIDs (0–1) | array |
| `agr` | heading ("action group") UUIDs (0–1) | array |
| `tg` | tag UUIDs | array |
| `ix` | sort index in container | int |
| `ti` | Today-view sort index | int |
| `sb` | evening bit: 1=This Evening | int |
| `do` | due-date offset (usually 0) | int |
| `rr` | recurrence rule object (below) or null | obj |
| `rt` | recurrence template UUID (instance→template link) | array |
| `rmd`, `rp` | reminder/repeater metadata (null in normal creates) | |
| `icsd` | instance-creation start/suppressed day | int\|null |
| `acrd` | after-completion reference day | int\|null |
| `icc` | checklist/instance count | int |
| `icp` | instance creation paused | bool |
| `ato` | alarm time offset, seconds from midnight | int\|null |
| `lai` | last alarm interaction | float\|null |
| `lt` | leaves tombstone on delete | bool |
| `tr` | trashed | bool |
| `dl` | delegate/deadline-list (empty) | array |
| `xx` | CRDT conflict metadata, send `{"sn":{},"_t":"oo"}` on create | obj |
| `cd` / `md` | creation / modification timestamps | float |

**ChecklistItem3**: `tt`, `ss`, `sp`, `ts` (= parent task UUIDs array), `ix`, `cd`, `md`, `lt`, `xx` (`wire/checklist.rs`).
**Tag4**: `tt`, `sh` (keyboard shortcut), `ix`, `pn` (parent tag UUIDs — nesting), `xx` (`wire/tags.rs`).
**Area3**: `tt`, `tg`, `ix`, `xx` (`wire/area.rs`).
**Tombstone2** (`t:0`): `dloid` (deleted object UUID), `dld` (delete timestamp float) (`wire/tombstone.rs`).

**Recurrence rule (`rr`)** (`wire/recurrence.rs`): `{"tp":0|1 (fixed|after-completion), "fu":8|16|256 (daily|monthly|weekly bitmask), "fa":N (every N units), "of":[{weekday/day/ordinal selectors}], "sr":day, "ia":day, "ed":64092211200 (≈never), "rc":repeat-count, "ts":skip, "rrv":4}`.

**UUIDs — critical trap** (arthursoares Bug 5/9): IDs are 16-byte UUIDs encoded as **canonical Bitcoin Base58** (alphabet `123456789ABCDEFGHJKLMNPQRSTUVWXYZabcdefghijkmnopqrstuvwxyz`), 21–22 chars, with one leading `1` per leading zero byte. The server accepts any string, but Things.app decodes with `BSIdentifierFromBase58String()` — one malformed UUID **permanently corrupts the history and crashes every real client forever**. Don't use `shortuuid` defaults or plain UUID strings; implement canonical Base58 exactly (see `base58.go`). Use a throwaway Things Cloud account while developing.

## 4. Writes / commits

```
POST /version/1/history/{history-key}/commit?ancestor-index={current-head}&_cnt=1
Push-Priority: 5      (Things Mac uses 5 or 10)
+ headers from §1
Body: {"<uuid>": {"t":0,"e":"Task6","p":{...full snapshot...}},
       "<uuid2>": {"t":1,"e":"Task6","p":{...sparse patch...}}}
```

200 → `{"server-head-index": N}`; store N as your new head for the next commit's `ancestor-index`. `ancestor-index` must be the head you last saw (get it from a fresh `/items` fetch — its `current-item-index` — or `GET /history/{key}`'s `latest-server-index`). Multiple objects per commit are fine (that's how task+checklist-items are created atomically), but a body map can hold only **one op per UUID**.

Conflict handling: none of the four implementations special-cases a 409 — they all treat non-200 as a generic error and the safe pattern is "sync to head immediately before commit, retry the whole read-modify-commit on failure." Since the journal is append-only with per-field patches, semantic conflicts are resolved by last-writer-wins on fold order (plus `md` timestamps and the `xx` CRDT blob, which clients just pass through). Known concrete status codes: 500 for out-of-range `start-index`, 401 for bad credentials, 202 for deletes.

Semantic pitfalls that crash the real app (all HAR-verified in `docs/client-side-bugs.md`): never send `st:2` with today's date (Today = `st:1` + `sr`=`tir`=today-midnight-UTC); tasks placed in a project/heading/area should get `st:1` not `st:0`; headings/projects must never have `st:0`; send `cd`/`md` as fractional floats.

## 5. Date encoding

Two encodings, no packed "Things date" ints on the wire (that's the local SQLite format):
- **Precise timestamps** (`cd`, `md`, `sp`, `dld`, `lai`): unix epoch seconds as **float with fractional part** (e.g. `1378062649.570914`). Integer truncation risks ordering bugs.
- **Day timestamps** (`sr`, `tir`, `dd`, `dds`, `icsd`, `acrd`, `rr.sr/ia/ed`): unix epoch seconds at **00:00:00 UTC of the calendar day**, as integer (e.g. `1774396800` = 2026-03-25 00:00 UTC; always divisible by 86400).
- `ato` (reminder time): seconds since midnight. `rr.ed` "never" sentinel: `64092211200` (year 4001).

## 6. Recommendation

Base your Python CLI on **evanpurkhiser/things3-cloud** for the wire model/client flow (it's the most current, actively maintained as of last week, and its `trycmd/*/journal.json` fixtures give you hundreds of real payload examples), cross-checked against **arthursoares/things-cloud-sdk** `docs/client-side-bugs.md` before writing anything to a real account. Protocol stability: schema 301 and all `/version/1/...` endpoints unchanged since ~2017; entity versions bumped over the years (Task4→Task6, ChecklistItem2→3, Tag3→4, Area2→3, Tombstone→2) but current clients still read the legacy ones, and notes migrated from XML strings to the structured `{"_t":"tx"}` object. The one live risk is not the protocol but write hygiene — malformed Base58 UUIDs or invalid `st`/date combinations poison the shared history irreversibly.

Sources: [evanpurkhiser/things3-cloud](https://github.com/evanpurkhiser/things3-cloud), [arthursoares/things-cloud-sdk](https://github.com/arthursoares/things-cloud-sdk), [nicolai86/things-cloud-sdk](https://github.com/nicolai86/things-cloud-sdk), [disrupted/things-cloud-api](https://github.com/disrupted/things-cloud-api), [mattydsmith/things-cloud-mcp](https://github.com/mattydsmith/things-cloud-mcp), [m1guelpf/things-cloud-api](https://github.com/m1guelpf/things-cloud-api)</result>

**Tombstone2** (`t:0`): `dloid` (deleted object UUID), `dld` (delete timestamp float) (`wire/tombstone.rs`).

**Recurrence rule (`rr`)** (`wire/recurrence.rs`): `{"tp":0|1 (fixed|after-completion), "fu":8|16|256 (daily|monthly|weekly bitmask), "fa":N (every N units), "of":[{weekday/day/ordinal selectors}], "sr":day, "ia":day, "ed":64092211200 (≈never), "rc":repeat-count, "ts":skip, "rrv":4}`.

**UUIDs — critical trap** (arthursoares Bug 5/9): IDs are 16-byte UUIDs encoded as **canonical Bitcoin Base58** (alphabet `123456789ABCDEFGHJKLMNPQRSTUVWXYZabcdefghijkmnopqrstuvwxyz`), 21–22 chars, with one leading `1` per leading zero byte. The server accepts any string, but Things.app decodes with `BSIdentifierFromBase58String()` — one malformed UUID **permanently corrupts the history and crashes every real client forever**. Don't use `shortuuid` defaults or plain UUID strings; implement canonical Base58 exactly (see `base58.go`). Use a throwaway Things Cloud account while developing.

## 4. Writes / commits

```
POST /version/1/history/{history-key}/commit?ancestor-index={current-head}&_cnt=1
Push-Priority: 5      (Things Mac uses 5 or 10)
+ headers from §1
Body: {"<uuid>": {"t":0,"e":"Task6","p":{...full snapshot...}},
       "<uuid2>": {"t":1,"e":"Task6","p":{...sparse patch...}}}
```

200 → `{"server-head-index": N}`; store N as your new head for the next commit's `ancestor-index`. `ancestor-index` must be the head you last saw (get it from a fresh `/items` fetch — its `current-item-index` — or `GET /history/{key}`'s `latest-server-index`). Multiple objects per commit are fine (that's how task+checklist-items are created atomically), but a body map can hold only **one op per UUID**.

Conflict handling: none of the four implementations special-cases a 409 — they all treat non-200 as a generic error and the safe pattern is "sync to head immediately before commit, retry the whole read-modify-commit on failure." Since the journal is append-only with per-field patches, semantic conflicts are resolved by last-writer-wins on fold order (plus `md` timestamps and the `xx` CRDT blob, which clients just pass through). Known concrete status codes: 500 for out-of-range `start-index`, 401 for bad credentials, 202 for deletes.

Semantic pitfalls that crash the real app (all HAR-verified in `docs/client-side-bugs.md`): never send `st:2` with today's date (Today = `st:1` + `sr`=`tir`=today-midnight-UTC); tasks placed in a project/heading/area should get `st:1` not `st:0`; headings/projects must never have `st:0`; send `cd`/`md` as fractional floats.

## 5. Date encoding

Two encodings, no packed "Things date" ints on the wire (that's the local SQLite format):
- **Precise timestamps** (`cd`, `md`, `sp`, `dld`, `lai`): unix epoch seconds as **float with fractional part** (e.g. `1378062649.570914`). Integer truncation risks ordering bugs.
- **Day timestamps** (`sr`, `tir`, `dd`, `dds`, `icsd`, `acrd`, `rr.sr/ia/ed`): unix epoch seconds at **00:00:00 UTC of the calendar day**, as integer (e.g. `1774396800` = 2026-03-25 00:00 UTC; always divisible by 86400).
- `ato` (reminder time): seconds since midnight. `rr.ed` "never" sentinel: `64092211200` (year 4001).

## 6. Recommendation

Base your Python CLI on **evanpurkhiser/things3-cloud** for the wire model/client flow (it's the most current, actively maintained as of last week, and its `trycmd/*/journal.json` fixtures give you hundreds of real payload examples), cross-checked against **arthursoares/things-cloud-sdk** `docs/client-side-bugs.md` before writing anything to a real account. Protocol stability: schema 301 and all `/version/1/...` endpoints unchanged since ~2017; entity versions bumped over the years (Task4→Task6, ChecklistItem2→3, Tag3→4, Area2→3, Tombstone→2) but current clients still read the legacy ones, and notes migrated from XML strings to the structured `{"_t":"tx"}` object. The one live risk is not the protocol but write hygiene — malformed Base58 UUIDs or invalid `st`/date combinations poison the shared history irreversibly.

Sources: [evanpurkhiser/things3-cloud](https://github.com/evanpurkhiser/things3-cloud), [arthursoares/things-cloud-sdk](https://github.com/arthursoares/things-cloud-sdk), [nicolai86/things-cloud-sdk](https://github.com/nicolai86/things-cloud-sdk), [disrupted/things-cloud-api](https://github.com/disrupted/things-cloud-api), [mattydsmith/things-cloud-mcp](https://github.com/mattydsmith/things-cloud-mcp), [m1guelpf/things-cloud-api](https://github.com/m1guelpf/things-cloud-api)</result>

content.text: [Request interrupted by user]
awesome. so whats next

The research came back solid — the protocol is fully mapped (endpoints, `Task6` field codes, commit flow, the Base58-UUID trap), with two actively-maintained reference implementations. Next is the actual rebuild, in this order:

1. **Pull the two reference repos** (evanpurkhiser/things3-cloud in Rust, arthursoares/things-cloud-sdk in Go) into my scratchpad and read the load-bearing files — the client, the Task6 wire model, the canonical Base58 codec, and the HAR-verified pitfalls doc — so the Python port copies verified behavior, not my paraphrase of it.
2. **Rewrite the package in the tt mold**: `api.py` (login → history-key, items pagination, commit), `base58.py` (canonical codec — the one thing that can corrupt a history if wrong), `sync.py` (fold items into todos/projects/headings/checklists/areas/tags + `~/.cache` cursor cache + write-op wire builders), adapted `models.py` and `cli.py`. The SQLite/URL-scheme/AppleScript modules I started earlier get deleted.
3. **Offline tests**: Base58 round-trips against known-good vectors from the reference repos, day-timestamp/date encoding, and journal-fold tests on fixture payloads.
4. **Wire up auth** the tt way: `THINGS_CLOUD_EMAIL`/`THINGS_CLOUD_PASSWORD` from `~/.envrc` → `things auth` saves the fetched history-key to `~/.config/things-cli/auth.json`; `things doctor` verifies. Live read verification then works from any Mac — no Things app needed, which is the whole point.

One thing I'll flag now rather than after: **writes to Things Cloud are genuinely riskier than reads** — a malformed UUID or invalid field combo poisons the shared history permanently and crashes the real apps. I'll implement writes with the verified invariants and `--dry-run` everywhere, but I'd strongly suggest first exercising them against a throwaway Things Cloud account before your real one. I'll note that in the README too.

Starting with step 1.

323	            && self.leaves_tombstone.is_none()
324	            && self.trashed.is_none()
325	            && self.creation_date.is_none()
326	            && self.modification_date.is_none()
327	    }
328	
329	    pub fn into_properties(self) -> BTreeMap<String, Value> {
330	        match serde_json::to_value(self) {
331	            Ok(Value::Object(map)) => map.into_iter().collect(),
332	            _ => BTreeMap::new(),
333	        }
334	    }
335	}
336	
337	/// Task kind used in `tp`.
338	#[derive(
339	    Debug,
340	    Clone,
341	    Copy,
342	    Serialize,
343	    Deserialize,
344	    PartialEq,
345	    Eq,
346	    Display,
347	    EnumString,
348	    FromPrimitive,
349	    IntoPrimitive,
350	)]
351	#[repr(i32)]
352	#[serde(from = "i32", into = "i32")]
353	pub enum TaskType {
354	    /// Regular leaf task.
355	    Todo = 0,
356	    /// Project container.
357	    Project = 1,
358	    /// Heading/section under a project.
359	    Heading = 2,
360	
361	    /// Unknown value preserved for forward compatibility.
362	    #[num_enum(catch_all)]
363	    #[strum(disabled, to_string = "{0}")]
364	    Unknown(i32),
365	}
366	
367	impl Default for TaskType {
368	    fn default() -> Self {
369	        Self::Todo
370	    }
371	}
372	
373	/// Task status used in `ss`.
374	#[derive(
375	    Debug,
376	    Clone,
377	    Copy,
378	    Serialize,
379	    Deserialize,
380	    PartialEq,
381	    Eq,
382	    Display,
383	    EnumString,
384	    FromPrimitive,
385	    IntoPrimitive,
386	)]

389	pub enum TaskStatus {
390	    /// Open/incomplete.
391	    Incomplete = 0,
392	    /// Canceled.
393	    Canceled = 2,
394	    /// Completed.
395	    Completed = 3,
396	
397	    /// Unknown value preserved for forward compatibility.
398	    #[num_enum(catch_all)]
399	    #[strum(disabled, to_string = "{0}")]
400	    Unknown(i32),
401	}
402	
403	impl Default for TaskStatus {
404	    fn default() -> Self {
405	        Self::Incomplete
406	    }
407	}

410	#[derive(
411	    Debug,
412	    Clone,
413	    Copy,

418	    Display,

422	)]
423	#[repr(i32)]

425	pub enum TaskStart {

427	    Inbox = 0,
428	    /// Anytime list.

1	# Client-Side Bugs — Things App Crash Investigation
2	
3	## Context
4	
5	After updating the SDK and building `things-cli`, tasks created by the CLI caused the Things macOS app to crash or behave erratically upon sync. The root causes were identified by comparing `things-cli` output against a Proxyman HAR capture of the real Things 3.15 client (`assets/Things_02-10-2026-10-01-09.har`).
6	
7	## Bug 1: Schedule Field (`st`) Values Were Swapped (CRITICAL)
8	
9	### What `st` actually means
10	
11	The `st` JSON field maps to the `start` column (row 11) in Things' internal SQLite database. It is NOT a "schedule type" — it represents the **start state** of a task. The Things UI determines which view to show the task in based on the combination of `st` and the `sr`/`tir` date fields:
12	
13	| `st` | Start state | `sr`/`tir` | Things UI view |
14	|------|-------------|------------|----------------|
15	| 0    | Not started | null       | **Inbox**      |
16	| 1    | Started     | today's date | **Today**    |
17	| 1    | Started     | null       | **Anytime**    |
18	| 2    | Deferred    | future date | **Upcoming**  |
19	| 2    | Deferred    | null       | **Someday**    |
20	
21	### What things-cli was sending (WRONG)
22	
23	```go
24	case "today":
25	    st = 2    // WRONG — 2 means "deferred" (someday)
26	case "someday", "anytime":
27	    st = 1    // WRONG for someday — 1 means "started" (anytime)
28	```
29	
30	This produced an **invalid combination**: `st=2` (deferred) paired with `sr`/`tir` set to today's date. The Things app does not expect a "deferred" task to have today's date — this state has no valid UI representation and caused the client to crash.
31	
32	### HAR evidence
33	
34	The real Things app creates a "Today" task with:
35	```json
36	{
37	  "st": 1,
38	  "sr": 1770681600,
39	  "tir": 1770681600
40	}
41	```
42	Where `1770681600` is 2026-02-10 00:00:00 UTC (the day of capture).
43	
44	All 5 instances of `st=1` with dates in the HAR used today's date. All 5 instances of `st=2` used future dates or null (someday).
45	
46	### Fix
47	
48	```go
49	case "today":
50	    st = 1  // "started" — combined with today's sr/tir = Today view
51	case "anytime":
52	    st = 1  // "started" — without dates = Anytime view
53	case "someday":
54	    st = 2  // "deferred" — without dates = Someday view
55	case "inbox":
56	    st = 0  // "not started" — Inbox view
57	```
58	
59	### Also affected: `listTasks` today filter
60	
61	The `things-cli today` command filtered tasks by `task.Schedule != 2`, which matched "someday" tasks, not "today" tasks. Fixed to check `task.Schedule == 1` AND `task.ScheduledDate == today`.
62	
63	## Bug 2: SDK `TaskSchedule` Constants Were Misleading
64	
65	### The problem
66	
67	The SDK constants named value 0 as "Today":
68	
69	```go
70	TaskScheduleToday   TaskSchedule = 0  // Actually Inbox!
71	TaskScheduleAnytime TaskSchedule = 1  // Correct
72	TaskScheduleSomeday TaskSchedule = 2  // Correct
73	```
74	
75	This naming was incorrect. The value `0` maps to "not started" = **Inbox**, not Today. Today tasks use `st=1` (same as Anytime) differentiated by having `sr`/`tir` dates set.
76	
77	### Fix
78	
79	Renamed to reflect actual semantics:
80	
81	```go
82	TaskScheduleInbox   TaskSchedule = 0  // Not started
83	TaskScheduleAnytime TaskSchedule = 1  // Started (Today when sr/tir=today, Anytime when null)
84	TaskScheduleSomeday TaskSchedule = 2  // Deferred
85	
86	// Deprecated alias for backward compatibility
87	TaskScheduleToday = TaskScheduleInbox
88	```
89	
90	## Bug 3: Timestamp Precision Loss
91	
92	### The problem
93	
94	`Timestamp.MarshalJSON()` serialized as integer seconds:
95	
96	```go
97	func (t *Timestamp) MarshalJSON() ([]byte, error) {
98	    var tt = time.Time(*t).Unix()  // truncates to integer
99	    return json.Marshal(tt)        // outputs: 1496009117
100	}
101	```
102	
103	The real Things API uses **fractional seconds** (e.g., `1770713623.4716659` for `cd`/`md` fields). The integer truncation could cause ordering issues when Things compares modification timestamps for conflict resolution.
104	
105	Similarly, `UnmarshalJSON` was discarding sub-second precision:
106	```go
107	*t = Timestamp(time.Unix(int64(d), 0).UTC())  // nanoseconds always 0
108	```
109	
110	### Fix
111	
112	Both methods now preserve nanosecond precision:
113	
114	```go
115	// Marshal: fractional epoch output
116	func (t *Timestamp) MarshalJSON() ([]byte, error) {
117	    tt := time.Time(*t)
118	    ts := float64(tt.UnixNano()) / 1e9
119	    return json.Marshal(ts)
120	}
121	
122	// Unmarshal: preserve fractional part
123	sec := int64(d)
124	nsec := int64((d - float64(sec)) * 1e9)
125	*t = Timestamp(time.Unix(sec, nsec).UTC())
126	```
127	
128	## Bug 4: State Aggregation Crashes (Server-Side Processing)
129	
130	Two additional bugs in the SDK's state aggregation layer caused panics when processing the event stream:
131	
132	### 4a: Nil pointer in `hasArea()` (state/memory/memory.go)
133	
134	When a parent task was deleted (via tombstone or `ItemActionDeleted`), child tasks still referenced the parent UUID in `ParentTaskIDs`. The recursive `hasArea()` lookup followed `state.Tasks[parentID]` which returned `nil`, then accessed `.AreaIDs` — panic.
135	
136	**Fix:** Added `if task == nil { return false }` nil guard.
137	
138	### 4b: Slice out-of-bounds in `ApplyPatches()` (notes.go)
139	
140	When a note's delta patch had `Position` exceeding the current string length (possible when patches arrive out of order during sync), the slice operation `runes[:p.Position]` panicked.
141	
142	**Fix:** Clamped `Position` and `end` to `len(runes)` before slicing.
143	
144	## Verification Method
145	
146	All bugs were identified by:
147	1. Capturing the real Things 3.15 client traffic via Proxyman (91 requests)
148	2. Extracting all POST commit payloads from the HAR file
149	3. Comparing field values (especially `st`, `sr`, `tir`) between the HAR capture and `things-cli` debug output
150	4. Cross-referencing against the Things SQLite schema comments in `types.go`
151	
152	## Bug 5: Things.app Crashes on Cloud-Synced Tasks — RESOLVED
153	
154	### Root Cause: UUIDs Were Not Base58-Encoded
155	
156	The crash was **not** caused by notes, `md` timestamps, field ordering, or the two-commit pattern. **The root cause was invalid UUID encoding.**
157	
158	Things.app's `BSSyncValueEncoder.decode()` in `Base.framework` calls `BSIdentifierFromBase58String()` to parse each UUID key from sync data. The SDK was generating UUIDs using a fake Base62 mapping (modulo of raw bytes against an alphanumeric alphabet), which produced strings that **looked** plausible but were **not valid Base58**.
159	
160	When Things.app tried to decode these UUIDs:
161	1. `BSIdentifierFromBase58String()` returned an invalid result
162	2. The decode loop's array bounds check failed
163	3. `EXC_BREAKPOINT` (Swift `brk #1`) at Base.framework offset `0xA6194`
164	
165	### How We Found It
166	
167	1. **Crash report analysis** (`~/Library/Logs/DiagnosticReports/Things3-2026-02-10-124613.ips`) — the crash was in `Base.BSSyncValueEncoder.decode()`, NOT `insertTaskWithUUID:usingTombstones:` as initially assumed
168	2. **Hopper disassembly** of `Base.framework` — traced the crash to the `BSIdentifierFromBase58String()` call and its bounds-check trap
169	3. **Register state** at crash — `x23 = 0xFFFFFFFF` (error sentinel from failed Base58 decode), `x22` non-zero (ruling out the nil-operation path)
170	4. **HAR capture comparison** — real Things UUIDs are 21-22 character Base58 strings (e.g., `Q9sihFX2SsvGaz6vv4J2Hf`), not standard UUID format
171	
172	### The Fix
173	
174	Replace the broken UUID generation:
175	
176	```go
177	// BEFORE (broken — fake Base62, not valid Base58):
178	chars := "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZabcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz0123456789"
179	result := make([]byte, 22)
180	for i := 0; i < 22; i++ {
181	    result[i] = chars[int(bytes[i%16])%len(chars)]
182	}
183	
184	// AFTER (correct — proper Base58 encoding with Bitcoin alphabet):
185	const alphabet = "123456789ABCDEFGHJKLMNPQRSTUVWXYZabcdefghijkmnopqrstuvwxyz"
186	n := new(big.Int).SetBytes(uuid[:])
187	base := big.NewInt(58)
188	for n.Sign() > 0 {
189	    n.DivMod(n, base, mod)
190	    encoded = append(encoded, alphabet[mod.Int64()])
191	}
192	// reverse for big-endian
193	```
194	
195	Key differences:
196	- Base58 alphabet excludes `0`, `O`, `I`, `l` (avoids visual ambiguity)
197	- Proper base conversion via `math/big` division, not modulo mapping
198	- Produces 21-22 character strings matching Things' native format
199	
200	### Verification
201	
202	Tested on account **things33** (2026-02-10):
203	- Task creation (no note) — syncs and displays correctly
204	- Task creation with note (single commit) — syncs and displays correctly
205	- Task editing, completion, trashing — all work
206	- Project with headings and subtasks — all work
207	- Tag and area creation — all work
208	
209	The "note bug" was a red herring. Notes in single-commit creates work fine — every test that "failed on notes" actually failed because the UUID was invalid.
210	
211	### Why Corrupted Accounts Cascade-Failed
212	
213	Once an item with an invalid UUID is written to the cloud history, **every subsequent sync attempt crashes**. The server stores UUID keys as opaque strings, so it accepts anything. But the client must decode every UUID in the history during sync. One bad UUID poisons the entire history, which is why things9-23 all became permanently unusable.
214	
215	## Bug 6: Tasks Under Projects/Headings/Areas Default to Inbox
216	
217	### The Problem
218	
219	When creating a task with `--project`, `--heading`, or `--area`, the CLI defaulted to `st=0` (inbox). This caused tasks to appear in the Inbox instead of under their parent project/heading/area in Things.app.
220	
221	### Root Cause
222	
223	Tasks placed into a project, under a heading, or within an area have already been "triaged" — they shouldn't land in Inbox. The real Things app creates these tasks with `st=1` (anytime/started).
224	
225	### Fix
226	
227	Auto-set `st=1` when `--project`, `--heading`, or `--area` is provided, unless `--when` is explicitly set:
228	
229	```go
230	// --project
231	if v, ok := opts["project"]; ok && v != "" {
232	    pr = []string{v}
233	    if _, hasWhen := opts["when"]; !hasWhen {
234	        st = 1
235	    }
236	}
237	// Same pattern for --heading and --area
238	```
239	
240	This follows the same principle as the heading fix (Bug 5): structural elements inside projects and areas are never "inbox" items.
241	
242	## Bug 7: Incremental Sync Returns 500 Errors (2026-02-11)
243	
244	### The Problem
245	
246	The persistent sync engine (`sync` package) was getting 500 errors on incremental syncs when fetching items from Things Cloud. The error occurred on the second or third batch of items, not the first.
247	
248	### Root Cause
249	
250	The server returns 500 when `start-index > current-item-index` (out of bounds request). The SDK was calculating the next page's start index incorrectly.
251	
252	Items in the `/items` response are nested maps:
253	```json
254	{
255	  "current-item-index": 201,
256	  "items": [
257	    {"uuid1": {...}, "uuid2": {...}},  // 1 server item, 2 entities
258	    {"uuid3": {...}},                   // 1 server item, 1 entity
259	    ...
260	  ]
261	}
262	```
263	
264	During parsing, these nested maps get *expanded* — a single server item with multiple entity keys becomes multiple `Item` structs. The SDK was using `len(expandedItems)` to calculate the next start index, but the server's `current-item-index` advances by the *outer array* count.
265	
266	**Example**: Request `start-index=177` returns 24 server items that expand to 32 entities, with `current-item-index=201`.
267	- Wrong: `177 + 32 = 209` → server returns 500 (209 > 201)
268	- Right: use server's `current-item-index` directly → 201
269	
270	### The Fix
271	
272	Two changes to `sync/sync.go`:
273	
274	1. **Pre-check**: Call `GET /history/{id}` to get `latest-server-index` before fetching items. Skip if stored cursor >= server index (nothing new to fetch).
275	
276	2. **Pagination**: Use `s.history.LatestServerIndex` (populated from response's `current-item-index`) instead of calculating `startIndex + len(items)`.
277	
278	```go
279	// Before (wrong):
280	startIndex = startIndex + len(items)  // items is expanded count
281	
282	// After (correct):
283	startIndex = s.history.LatestServerIndex  // server's actual index
284	```
285	
286	### Additional Discovery
287	
288	The `/items` endpoint doesn't require authentication — you can curl it directly. Things.app also caches the history ID locally and skips the `/account/{email}` call on incremental syncs. The SDK now matches this pattern.
289	
290	### Verification
291	
292	Tested on account **things36** (2026-02-11):
293	- Fresh sync from index 0 — works
294	- Incremental sync — works (no 500)
295	- Multiple consecutive syncs — works
296	- After external changes in Things.app — detects and fetches delta correctly
297	
298	## Bug 8: Edit Command Doesn't Set st=1 When Adding Project/Area/Heading (2026-02-12)
299	
300	### The Problem
301	
302	The `things-cli edit` command's `--project`, `--area`, and `--heading` flags only set their respective fields without updating `st` (schedule). This caused tasks to remain in Inbox (`st=0`) instead of moving to Anytime (`st=1`) when organizing via edit.
303	
304	### Root Cause
305	
306	The edit path was incomplete compared to the create path. When creating a task with `--project`/`--area`/`--heading`, the CLI auto-sets `st=1`. But when editing an existing task to add these, only the parent reference was being updated.
307	
308	### Evidence
309	
310	HAR capture (`assets/task_inbox-to-project.har`) shows Things.app sends both fields when moving a task from Inbox to a project:
311	
312	```json
313	{"pr":["BVU8qZ9dNjrdxLvDHPvfDS"],"st":1,"ix":4712,"md":...}
314	```
315	
316	### The Fix
317	
318	Updated `cmdEdit()` to set `st=1` (Anytime) when `--project`, `--area`, or `--heading` is provided, unless `--when` is explicitly set:
319	
320	```go
321	if v, ok := opts["area"]; ok && v != "" {
322	    u.Area(v)
323	    if _, hasWhen := opts["when"]; !hasWhen {
324	        u.Schedule(1, 0, 0) // Anytime
325	    }
326	}
327	// Same pattern for --project and --heading
328	```
329	
330	### Verification
331	
332	Tasks moved from Inbox to project/area/heading via `things-cli edit` now correctly appear under their parent instead of remaining in Inbox.
333	
334	## Bug 9: Base58 Encoder Dropped Leading Zero Bytes (2026-07-06)
335	
336	### The Problem
337	
338	Even after the Bug 5 fix switched UUID generation to Base58, accounts still intermittently accumulated items that crashed Things.app. The failure was probabilistic: everything worked for dozens of creates, then one item poisoned the history.
339	
340	### Root Cause
341	
342	The Base58 encoders in `things-cli` and `example/` (duplicated code) converted the UUID through `math/big`, which normalizes away leading zero bytes. Canonical Base58 represents each leading `0x00` byte as a leading `1` character; without that padding, a UUID whose first byte is zero encodes one character short and no longer decodes to 16 bytes. Byte 0 of a v4 UUID is fully random, so **~1 in 256 generated identifiers was malformed** — feeding the exact decoder (`BSIdentifierFromBase58String`, see Bug 5) whose array-bounds failure crashes the app.
343	
344	### Evidence
345	
346	- All identifiers captured from real Things.app traffic round-trip through a canonical encoder unchanged (`VJ1edXTP9q3PmFDUuy8EQh`, `FQxaqvLBkbR5q2Q5oRoknc`, `BVU8qZ9dNjrdxLvDHPvfDS`) — Things uses standard Bitcoin-style Base58 with leading-`1` padding.
347	- Empirical: a UUID starting `0x00` encoded by the old code round-trips to only 15 bytes.
348	
349	### The Fix (v0.3.0)
350	
351	Moved a single canonical implementation into the SDK (`base58.go`): `EncodeUUID` emits one `1` per leading zero byte; `DecodeUUID` rejects anything that doesn't decode to exactly 16 bytes; `NewUUID()` is the one true generator. Defense in depth:
352	
353	- `History.Write()` validates every item UUID (and rejects duplicate UUIDs per commit) before anything reaches the server
354	- `things-cli` validates all user-supplied identifiers (`--uuid`, `--project`, `--area`, `--heading`, `--tags`, positional targets)
355	- Related hole closed: `create --type heading --when inbox` could still emit `tp=2, st=0` (Bug 6's crash payload) because the `--when` switch ran after the type switch; projects and headings now force `st=1` after all schedule processing
356	
357	### Verification
358	
359	TDD with real-traffic round-trip vectors; forced leading-zero-byte cases in the round-trip suite; 2000-draw canonical-validity test on the generator.
360	
361	## Files Changed
362	
363	| File | Changes |
364	|------|---------|
365	| `cmd/things-cli/main.go` | Fixed `st` values, fixed `generateUUID()` to use Base58, added `create-area` command, auto-set `st=1` for --project/--heading/--area (create and edit - Bug 8), added `batch` command for multiple ops in one HTTP request |
366	| `syncutil/syncutil.go` | New package with shared utilities for sync-based CLI tools |
367	| `example/main.go` | Base58 UUID encoding, removed `ModificationDate` from creates |
368	| `types.go` | Renamed `TaskScheduleToday` → `TaskScheduleInbox`, fixed `Timestamp` marshal/unmarshal |
369	| `types_test.go` | Updated marshal test for fractional output |
370	| `itemaction_string.go` | Updated stringer for renamed constant |
371	| `state/memory/memory.go` | Nil guard in `hasArea()` |
372	| `notes.go` | Bounds clamping in `ApplyPatches()` |
373	| `notes_test.go` | Regression tests for edge cases |
374	| `state/memory/memory_test.go` | Regression tests for tombstone and orphan cases |
375	| `sync/sync.go` | Pre-check server index, fix pagination to use server's `current-item-index`, added `getServerIndex()` |
376	| `histories.go` | Added `HistoryWithID()` helper for cached history ID |
377

# Client-Side Bugs — Things App Crash Investigation

## Context

After updating the SDK and building `things-cli`, tasks created by the CLI caused the Things macOS app to crash or behave erratically upon sync. The root causes were identified by comparing `things-cli` output against a Proxyman HAR capture of the real Things 3.15 client (`assets/Things_02-10-2026-10-01-09.har`).

## Bug 1: Schedule Field (`st`) Values Were Swapped (CRITICAL)

### What `st` actually means

The `st` JSON field maps to the `start` column (row 11) in Things' internal SQLite database. It is NOT a "schedule type" — it represents the **start state** of a task. The Things UI determines which view to show the task in based on the combination of `st` and the `sr`/`tir` date fields:

| `st` | Start state | `sr`/`tir` | Things UI view |
|------|-------------|------------|----------------|
| 0    | Not started | null       | **Inbox**      |
| 1    | Started     | today's date | **Today**    |
| 1    | Started     | null       | **Anytime**    |
| 2    | Deferred    | future date | **Upcoming**  |
| 2    | Deferred    | null       | **Someday**    |

### What things-cli was sending (WRONG)

```go
case "today":
    st = 2    // WRONG — 2 means "deferred" (someday)
case "someday", "anytime":
    st = 1    // WRONG for someday — 1 means "started" (anytime)
```

This produced an **invalid combination**: `st=2` (deferred) paired with `sr`/`tir` set to today's date. The Things app does not expect a "deferred" task to have today's date — this state has no valid UI representation and caused the client to crash.

### HAR evidence

The real Things app creates a "Today" task with:
```json
{
  "st": 1,
  "sr": 1770681600,
  "tir": 1770681600
}
```
Where `1770681600` is 2026-02-10 00:00:00 UTC (the day of capture).

All 5 instances of `st=1` with dates in the HAR used today's date. All 5 instances of `st=2` used future dates or null (someday).

### Fix

```go
case "today":
    st = 1  // "started" — combined with today's sr/tir = Today view
case "anytime":
    st = 1  // "started" — without dates = Anytime view
case "someday":
    st = 2  // "deferred" — without dates = Someday view
case "inbox":
    st = 0  // "not started" — Inbox view
```

### Also affected: `listTasks` today filter

The `things-cli today` command filtered tasks by `task.Schedule != 2`, which matched "someday" tasks, not "today" tasks. Fixed to check `task.Schedule == 1` AND `task.ScheduledDate == today`.

## Bug 2: SDK `TaskSchedule` Constants Were Misleading

### The problem

The SDK constants named value 0 as "Today":

```go
TaskScheduleToday   TaskSchedule = 0  // Actually Inbox!
TaskScheduleAnytime TaskSchedule = 1  // Correct
TaskScheduleSomeday TaskSchedule = 2  // Correct
```

This naming was incorrect. The value `0` maps to "not started" = **Inbox**, not Today. Today tasks use `st=1` (same as Anytime) differentiated by having `sr`/`tir` dates set.

### Fix

Renamed to reflect actual semantics:

```go
TaskScheduleInbox   TaskSchedule = 0  // Not started
TaskScheduleAnytime TaskSchedule = 1  // Started (Today when sr/tir=today, Anytime when null)
TaskScheduleSomeday TaskSchedule = 2  // Deferred

// Deprecated alias for backward compatibility
TaskScheduleToday = TaskScheduleInbox
```

## Bug 3: Timestamp Precision Loss

### The problem

`Timestamp.MarshalJSON()` serialized as integer seconds:

```go
func (t *Timestamp) MarshalJSON() ([]byte, error) {
    var tt = time.Time(*t).Unix()  // truncates to integer
    return json.Marshal(tt)        // outputs: 1496009117
}
```

The real Things API uses **fractional seconds** (e.g., `1770713623.4716659` for `cd`/`md` fields). The integer truncation could cause ordering issues when Things compares modification timestamps for conflict resolution.

Similarly, `UnmarshalJSON` was discarding sub-second precision:
```go
*t = Timestamp(time.Unix(int64(d), 0).UTC())  // nanoseconds always 0
```

### Fix

Both methods now preserve nanosecond precision:

```go
// Marshal: fractional epoch output
func (t *Timestamp) MarshalJSON() ([]byte, error) {
    tt := time.Time(*t)
    ts := float64(tt.UnixNano()) / 1e9
    return json.Marshal(ts)
}

// Unmarshal: preserve fractional part
sec := int64(d)
nsec := int64((d - float64(sec)) * 1e9)
*t = Timestamp(time.Unix(sec, nsec).UTC())
```

## Bug 4: State Aggregation Crashes (Server-Side Processing)

Two additional bugs in the SDK's state aggregation layer caused panics when processing the event stream:

### 4a: Nil pointer in `hasArea()` (state/memory/memory.go)

When a parent task was deleted (via tombstone or `ItemActionDeleted`), child tasks still referenced the parent UUID in `ParentTaskIDs`. The recursive `hasArea()` lookup followed `state.Tasks[parentID]` which returned `nil`, then accessed `.AreaIDs` — panic.

**Fix:** Added `if task == nil { return false }` nil guard.

### 4b: Slice out-of-bounds in `ApplyPatches()` (notes.go)

When a note's delta patch had `Position` exceeding the current string length (possible when patches arrive out of order during sync), the slice operation `runes[:p.Position]` panicked.

**Fix:** Clamped `Position` and `end` to `len(runes)` before slicing.

## Verification Method

All bugs were identified by:
1. Capturing the real Things 3.15 client traffic via Proxyman (91 requests)
2. Extracting all POST commit payloads from the HAR file
3. Comparing field values (especially `st`, `sr`, `tir`) between the HAR capture and `things-cli` debug output
4. Cross-referencing against the Things SQLite schema comments in `types.go`

## Bug 5: Things.app Crashes on Cloud-Synced Tasks — RESOLVED

### Root Cause: UUIDs Were Not Base58-Encoded

The crash was **not** caused by notes, `md` timestamps, field ordering, or the two-commit pattern. **The root cause was invalid UUID encoding.**

Things.app's `BSSyncValueEncoder.decode()` in `Base.framework` calls `BSIdentifierFromBase58String()` to parse each UUID key from sync data. The SDK was generating UUIDs using a fake Base62 mapping (modulo of raw bytes against an alphanumeric alphabet), which produced strings that **looked** plausible but were **not valid Base58**.

When Things.app tried to decode these UUIDs:
1. `BSIdentifierFromBase58String()` returned an invalid result
2. The decode loop's array bounds check failed
3. `EXC_BREAKPOINT` (Swift `brk #1`) at Base.framework offset `0xA6194`

### How We Found It

1. **Crash report analysis** (`~/Library/Logs/DiagnosticReports/Things3-2026-02-10-124613.ips`) — the crash was in `Base.BSSyncValueEncoder.decode()`, NOT `insertTaskWithUUID:usingTombstones:` as initially assumed
2. **Hopper disassembly** of `Base.framework` — traced the crash to the `BSIdentifierFromBase58String()` call and its bounds-check trap
3. **Register state** at crash — `x23 = 0xFFFFFFFF` (error sentinel from failed Base58 decode), `x22` non-zero (ruling out the nil-operation path)
4. **HAR capture comparison** — real Things UUIDs are 21-22 character Base58 strings (e.g., `Q9sihFX2SsvGaz6vv4J2Hf`), not standard UUID format

### The Fix

Replace the broken UUID generation:

```go
// BEFORE (broken — fake Base62, not valid Base58):
chars := "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZabcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz0123456789"
result := make([]byte, 22)
for i := 0; i < 22; i++ {
    result[i] = chars[int(bytes[i%16])%len(chars)]
}

// AFTER (correct — proper Base58 encoding with Bitcoin alphabet):
const alphabet = "123456789ABCDEFGHJKLMNPQRSTUVWXYZabcdefghijkmnopqrstuvwxyz"
n := new(big.Int).SetBytes(uuid[:])
base := big.NewInt(58)
for n.Sign() > 0 {
    n.DivMod(n, base, mod)
    encoded = append(encoded, alphabet[mod.Int64()])
}
// reverse for big-endian
```

Key differences:
- Base58 alphabet excludes `0`, `O`, `I`, `l` (avoids visual ambiguity)
- Proper base conversion via `math/big` division, not modulo mapping
- Produces 21-22 character strings matching Things' native format

### Verification

Tested on account **things33** (2026-02-10):
- Task creation (no note) — syncs and displays correctly
- Task creation with note (single commit) — syncs and displays correctly
- Task editing, completion, trashing — all work
- Project with headings and subtasks — all work
- Tag and area creation — all work

The "note bug" was a red herring. Notes in single-commit creates work fine — every test that "failed on notes" actually failed because the UUID was invalid.

### Why Corrupted Accounts Cascade-Failed

Once an item with an invalid UUID is written to the cloud history, **every subsequent sync attempt crashes**. The server stores UUID keys as opaque strings, so it accepts anything. But the client must decode every UUID in the history during sync. One bad UUID poisons the entire history, which is why things9-23 all became permanently unusable.

## Bug 6: Tasks Under Projects/Headings/Areas Default to Inbox

### The Problem

When creating a task with `--project`, `--heading`, or `--area`, the CLI defaulted to `st=0` (inbox). This caused tasks to appear in the Inbox instead of under their parent project/heading/area in Things.app.

### Root Cause

Tasks placed into a project, under a heading, or within an area have already been "triaged" — they shouldn't land in Inbox. The real Things app creates these tasks with `st=1` (anytime/started).

### Fix

Auto-set `st=1` when `--project`, `--heading`, or `--area` is provided, unless `--when` is explicitly set:

```go
// --project
if v, ok := opts["project"]; ok && v != "" {
    pr = []string{v}
    if _, hasWhen := opts["when"]; !hasWhen {
        st = 1
    }
}
// Same pattern for --heading and --area
```

This follows the same principle as the heading fix (Bug 5): structural elements inside projects and areas are never "inbox" items.

## Bug 7: Incremental Sync Returns 500 Errors (2026-02-11)

### The Problem

The persistent sync engine (`sync` package) was getting 500 errors on incremental syncs when fetching items from Things Cloud. The error occurred on the second or third batch of items, not the first.

### Root Cause

The server returns 500 when `start-index > current-item-index` (out of bounds request). The SDK was calculating the next page's start index incorrectly.

Items in the `/items` response are nested maps:
```json
{
  "current-item-index": 201,
  "items": [
    {"uuid1": {...}, "uuid2": {...}},  // 1 server item, 2 entities
    {"uuid3": {...}},                   // 1 server item, 1 entity
    ...
  ]
}
```

During parsing, these nested maps get *expanded* — a single server item with multiple entity keys becomes multiple `Item` structs. The SDK was using `len(expandedItems)` to calculate the next start index, but the server's `current-item-index` advances by the *outer array* count.

**Example**: Request `start-index=177` returns 24 server items that expand to 32 entities, with `current-item-index=201`.
- Wrong: `177 + 32 = 209` → server returns 500 (209 > 201)
- Right: use server's `current-item-index` directly → 201

### The Fix

Two changes to `sync/sync.go`:

1. **Pre-check**: Call `GET /history/{id}` to get `latest-server-index` before fetching items. Skip if stored cursor >= server index (nothing new to fetch).

2. **Pagination**: Use `s.history.LatestServerIndex` (populated from response's `current-item-index`) instead of calculating `startIndex + len(items)`.

```go
// Before (wrong):
startIndex = startIndex + len(items)  // items is expanded count

// After (correct):
startIndex = s.history.LatestServerIndex  // server's actual index
```

### Additional Discovery

The `/items` endpoint doesn't require authentication — you can curl it directly. Things.app also caches the history ID locally and skips the `/account/{email}` call on incremental syncs. The SDK now matches this pattern.

### Verification

Tested on account **things36** (2026-02-11):
- Fresh sync from index 0 — works
- Incremental sync — works (no 500)
- Multiple consecutive syncs — works
- After external changes in Things.app — detects and fetches delta correctly

## Bug 8: Edit Command Doesn't Set st=1 When Adding Project/Area/Heading (2026-02-12)

### The Problem

The `things-cli edit` command's `--project`, `--area`, and `--heading` flags only set their respective fields without updating `st` (schedule). This caused tasks to remain in Inbox (`st=0`) instead of moving to Anytime (`st=1`) when organizing via edit.

### Root Cause

The edit path was incomplete compared to the create path. When creating a task with `--project`/`--area`/`--heading`, the CLI auto-sets `st=1`. But when editing an existing task to add these, only the parent reference was being updated.

### Evidence

HAR capture (`assets/task_inbox-to-project.har`) shows Things.app sends both fields when moving a task from Inbox to a project:

```json
{"pr":["BVU8qZ9dNjrdxLvDHPvfDS"],"st":1,"ix":4712,"md":...}
```

### The Fix

Updated `cmdEdit()` to set `st=1` (Anytime) when `--project`, `--area`, or `--heading` is provided, unless `--when` is explicitly set:

```go
if v, ok := opts["area"]; ok && v != "" {
    u.Area(v)
    if _, hasWhen := opts["when"]; !hasWhen {
        u.Schedule(1, 0, 0) // Anytime
    }
}
// Same pattern for --project and --heading
```

### Verification

Tasks moved from Inbox to project/area/heading via `things-cli edit` now correctly appear under their parent instead of remaining in Inbox.

## Bug 9: Base58 Encoder Dropped Leading Zero Bytes (2026-07-06)

### The Problem

Even after the Bug 5 fix switched UUID generation to Base58, accounts still intermittently accumulated items that crashed Things.app. The failure was probabilistic: everything worked for dozens of creates, then one item poisoned the history.

### Root Cause

The Base58 encoders in `things-cli` and `example/` (duplicated code) converted the UUID through `math/big`, which normalizes away leading zero bytes. Canonical Base58 represents each leading `0x00` byte as a leading `1` character; without that padding, a UUID whose first byte is zero encodes one character short and no longer decodes to 16 bytes. Byte 0 of a v4 UUID is fully random, so **~1 in 256 generated identifiers was malformed** — feeding the exact decoder (`BSIdentifierFromBase58String`, see Bug 5) whose array-bounds failure crashes the app.

### Evidence

- All identifiers captured from real Things.app traffic round-trip through a canonical encoder unchanged (`VJ1edXTP9q3PmFDUuy8EQh`, `FQxaqvLBkbR5q2Q5oRoknc`, `BVU8qZ9dNjrdxLvDHPvfDS`) — Things uses standard Bitcoin-style Base58 with leading-`1` padding.
- Empirical: a UUID starting `0x00` encoded by the old code round-trips to only 15 bytes.

### The Fix (v0.3.0)

Moved a single canonical implementation into the SDK (`base58.go`): `EncodeUUID` emits one `1` per leading zero byte; `DecodeUUID` rejects anything that doesn't decode to exactly 16 bytes; `NewUUID()` is the one true generator. Defense in depth:

- `History.Write()` validates every item UUID (and rejects duplicate UUIDs per commit) before anything reaches the server
- `things-cli` validates all user-supplied identifiers (`--uuid`, `--project`, `--area`, `--heading`, `--tags`, positional targets)
- Related hole closed: `create --type heading --when inbox` could still emit `tp=2, st=0` (Bug 6's crash payload) because the `--when` switch ran after the type switch; projects and headings now force `st=1` after all schedule processing

### Verification

TDD with real-traffic round-trip vectors; forced leading-zero-byte cases in the round-trip suite; 2000-draw canonical-validity test on the generator.

## Files Changed

| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `cmd/things-cli/main.go` | Fixed `st` values, fixed `generateUUID()` to use Base58, added `create-area` command, auto-set `st=1` for --project/--heading/--area (create and edit - Bug 8), added `batch` command for multiple ops in one HTTP request |
| `syncutil/syncutil.go` | New package with shared utilities for sync-based CLI tools |
| `example/main.go` | Base58 UUID encoding, removed `ModificationDate` from creates |
| `types.go` | Renamed `TaskScheduleToday` → `TaskScheduleInbox`, fixed `Timestamp` marshal/unmarshal |
| `types_test.go` | Updated marshal test for fractional output |
| `itemaction_string.go` | Updated stringer for renamed constant |
| `state/memory/memory.go` | Nil guard in `hasArea()` |
| `notes.go` | Bounds clamping in `ApplyPatches()` |
| `notes_test.go` | Regression tests for edge cases |
| `state/memory/memory_test.go` | Regression tests for tombstone and orphan cases |
| `sync/sync.go` | Pre-check server index, fix pagination to use server's `current-item-index`, added `getServerIndex()` |
| `histories.go` | Added `HistoryWithID()` helper for cached history ID |

from __future__ import annotations

from dataclasses import asdict, dataclass
from datetime import date, datetime
from enum import Enum


class Status(str, Enum):
    OPEN = "open"
    COMPLETED = "completed"
    CANCELED = "canceled"


class StartBucket(int, Enum):
    """The wire `st` field — a task's start state. ANYTIME with a scheduled
    date that has arrived is Today; SOMEDAY with a future date is Upcoming."""

    INBOX = 0
    ANYTIME = 1
    SOMEDAY = 2


def _jsonable(v):
    if isinstance(v, Enum):
        return v.value
    if isinstance(v, (datetime, date)):
        return v.isoformat()
    if isinstance(v, tuple):
        return list(v)
    return v


def to_json_dict(obj) -> dict:
    return {k: _jsonable(v) for k, v in asdict(obj).items()}


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Todo:
    id: str
    title: str
    notes: str = ""
    status: Status = Status.OPEN
    start: StartBucket = StartBucket.INBOX
    when: date | None = None          # scheduled day (`sr`) — drives Today/Upcoming
    deadline: date | None = None
    evening: bool = False             # This Evening (`sb`)
    repeating: bool = False           # instance generated by a repeat template
    trashed: bool = False
    project_id: str | None = None
    project_title: str | None = None
    area_id: str | None = None
    area_title: str | None = None
    heading_id: str | None = None
    heading_title: str | None = None
    tags: tuple[str, ...] = ()
    created: datetime | None = None
    modified: datetime | None = None
    stopped: datetime | None = None   # completion/cancellation time
    index: int = 0
    today_index: int = 0
    checklist_total: int = 0
    checklist_open: int = 0


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Project:
    id: str
    title: str
    notes: str = ""
    status: Status = Status.OPEN
    when: date | None = None
    deadline: date | None = None
    trashed: bool = False
    area_id: str | None = None
    area_title: str | None = None
    tags: tuple[str, ...] = ()
    created: datetime | None = None
    modified: datetime | None = None
    stopped: datetime | None = None
    index: int = 0
    open_count: int = 0               # open, untrashed todos inside


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Heading:
    id: str
    title: str
    project_id: str | None = None
    project_title: str | None = None
    trashed: bool = False
    index: int = 0


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Area:
    id: str
    title: str
    tags: tuple[str, ...] = ()
    index: int = 0


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Tag:
    id: str
    title: str
    shortcut: str = ""
    parent_id: str | None = None
    index: int = 0


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ChecklistItem:
    id: str
    title: str
    completed: bool = False
    stopped: datetime | None = None
    index: int = 0

from __future__ import annotations

from dataclasses import asdict, dataclass
from datetime import date, datetime
from enum import Enum


class Status(str, Enum):
    OPEN = "open"
    COMPLETED = "completed"
    CANCELED = "canceled"


class StartBucket(int, Enum):
    """TMTask.start — which of Things' coarse buckets an item sits in.
    ANYTIME with a startDate is Today; SOMEDAY with a startDate is Upcoming."""

    INBOX = 0
    ANYTIME = 1
    SOMEDAY = 2


def _jsonable(v):
    if isinstance(v, Enum):
        return v.value
    if isinstance(v, (datetime, date)):
        return v.isoformat()
    if isinstance(v, tuple):
        return list(v)
    return v


def to_json_dict(obj) -> dict:
    return {k: _jsonable(v) for k, v in asdict(obj).items()}


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Todo:
    id: str
    title: str
    notes: str = ""
    status: Status = Status.OPEN
    start: StartBucket = StartBucket.ANYTIME
    when: date | None = None          # startDate — drives Today/Upcoming
    deadline: date | None = None
    evening: bool = False             # This Evening (only meaningful in Today)
    repeating: bool = False           # generated by a repeat rule
    trashed: bool = False
    project_id: str | None = None
    project_title: str | None = None
    area_id: str | None = None
    area_title: str | None = None
    heading_id: str | None = None
    heading_title: str | None = None
    tags: tuple[str, ...] = ()
    created: datetime | None = None
    modified: datetime | None = None
    stopped: datetime | None = None   # completion/cancellation time
    checklist_total: int = 0
    checklist_open: int = 0


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Project:
    id: str
    title: str
    notes: str = ""
    status: Status = Status.OPEN
    when: date | None = None
    deadline: date | None = None
    trashed: bool = False
    area_id: str | None = None
    area_title: str | None = None
    tags: tuple[str, ...] = ()
    created: datetime | None = None
    modified: datetime | None = None
    stopped: datetime | None = None
    open_count: int = 0               # open, untrashed todos inside


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Heading:
    id: str
    title: str
    project_id: str | None = None
    project_title: str | None = None

class Area:

wait one short question. why not just use https://github.com/evanpurkhiser/things3-cloud

what are we adding?

Honest answer: for a *CLI*, not much. things3-cloud already covers nearly everything I was about to write — inbox/today/upcoming/logbook views, new/edit/mark/schedule/delete/reorder, areas/projects/tags, dry-run, even a webserver mode — it's actively maintained (last push five days ago), and critically its **write path is battle-tested against real accounts**. A fresh Python port re-introduces exactly the risk class their bug log documents (one malformed UUID or bad `st` combo permanently corrupts your sync history). Rewriting it would mostly be duplicating a maintained project with a less-proven implementation.

What a Python rewrite *would* genuinely add:

1. **A Python library surface** — importable from Atlas on the mini. If the point of Things-Sync is automation (mirroring, scheduled jobs, like your Todoist/Timelines sync jobs), a library you can `import` beats shelling out to a Rust binary and parsing its output. This was also the shape of the old Things-Sync (a library, no CLI).
2. **Ecosystem fit** — tt/td-style UX, `--json` everywhere for agents, uv tool install, consistent with your other CLIs.
3. No Rust/cargo dependency in bootstrap.

That's the real trade. A middle path exists too: Python library for **reads** (pull + fold the journal — low risk, that part is simple) while **writes** shell out to things3-cloud's binary, piggybacking on their proven write hygiene.

content.text: [Request interrupted by user]
one question before we continue though. if evanpurkhiser/things3-cloud already handles the writes well. what is the risk exactly?

The risk isn't that Python is worse than Rust — it's that a **new implementation loses their accumulated field-testing**, and the failure mode is silent.

Concretely, three tiers:

**Fully mitigable — the Base58 UUID trap.** Their bug 9 is the sharpest example: dropping leading-zero padding malforms roughly 1 in 256 identifiers. It works dozens of times, then poisons the account. I've already ported the canonical encoder, and this is completely testable offline: round-trip vectors from real Things traffic, forced leading-zero cases, a few thousand draws through a validator. Once that's green, this tier is closed.

**Mostly mitigable — the documented field invariants.** `st=2` paired with today's date crashes the app. Tasks placed in a project/heading/area need `st=1`, never `st=0`. Headings and projects must never carry `st=0`. Timestamps must be fractional floats. None of these are in a spec — they were extracted from HAR captures of real traffic. I can port every one and enforce them at a single choke point before anything reaches a commit.

**The real exposure — the invariants nobody has hit yet.** Their bug doc is 376 lines of "we tried this and Things crashed." It records bugs *found*, not bugs *absent*. A port inherits their code but not the years of running it against live accounts.

What makes all of this sharper than normal bug risk is that **it's silent and irreversible**. The server stores UUID keys as opaque strings and accepts anything — you get a 200. You find out when Things.app crashes on the next sync, and by then the bad item is in an append-only journal with no delete and no rollback. It's a shared history, so the blast radius is every device on the account, not just the CLI.

The cost curve is front-loaded, though: get it right before the first real commit and it's largely settled after that.

So the two things I'd want in place before any write touches your actual account:

1. **A throwaway Things Cloud account for first writes.** Cheap, and it converts the unknown-unknowns from "permanent corruption" into "delete the account."
2. **Differential verification** — generate the same operation with things3-cloud's binary and diff its payload against mine. That upgrades "I believe this matches" into "byte-identical to the proven implementation," which is the strongest check available without live testing.

Reads carry none of this — worst case I render something wrong and re-pull. If you want, I can build reads first and get the whole CLI useful, then do writes behind the differential harness.
if we copy the approach?

Copying the approach is exactly the right instinct — it closes most of the third tier, because the fixes they absorbed over years get baked into my port whether or not they're written down anywhere. The unknown-unknowns become known-goods by construction.

What it doesn't eliminate is **transcription fidelity**. The risk shifts from "did I understand the protocol?" — hard to verify — to "did I copy it correctly?" — mechanically verifiable. That's a much better risk to hold.

There's one specific place a faithful-looking port goes wrong, and I hit it while reading their `task.rs`. Their update patches use `Option<Option<i64>>` with `skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none"`. That double wrapper is load-bearing:

- outer `None` → field **absent** from the JSON → "leave this alone"
- `Some(None)` → field present as **null** → "clear this field"
- `Some(Some(v))` → set it to `v`

Python collapses both empty cases to `None`. If I write the obvious `if value is not None: payload["sr"] = value`, I lose the ability to clear a date; if I write the other obvious thing, I silently null out every field I didn't pass on every update. So the port needs an explicit `UNSET` sentinel distinct from `None` at the patch layer. That single distinction is probably the highest-value thing to get right in the whole port, and it's invisible if you're reading their code casually.

The other asset copying gives us: their `trycmd/**/journal.json` fixtures are hundreds of real payloads sitting on disk. Those become offline test vectors — I can fold them and assert the resulting state without touching the network.

So the plan: port their code paths rather than the prose description, use an `UNSET` sentinel for patch semantics, test the fold against their fixtures, and diff my generated payloads against their binary's before any real write. Resuming now with `sync.py`.
